questions.question: What percentage of NZ businesses report 'shadow AI' — staff using unapproved AI tools without oversight?

answers.feedback: 52% of NZ businesses identify shadow AI as a problem. This is more than half, which is why governance — even lightweight governance — is so important.

questions.question: In the AI Forum NZ's 'AI Ready' pathway, what is the first practice a small business should establish?

answers.text: Build an AI system inventory
answers.feedback: The first AI Ready practice is establishing governance ownership — naming one person who is responsible for AI governance decisions. For many small businesses, this is the owner or a senior manager.

answers.text: Establish governance ownership — name one person responsible
answers.feedback: The first AI Ready practice is establishing governance ownership — naming one person who is responsible for AI governance decisions. For many small businesses, this is the owner or a senior manager.

answers.text: Conduct a full risk assessment
answers.feedback: The first AI Ready practice is establishing governance ownership — naming one person who is responsible for AI governance decisions. For many small businesses, this is the owner or a senior manager.

answers.text: Engage external consultants
answers.feedback: The first AI Ready practice is establishing governance ownership — naming one person who is responsible for AI governance decisions. For many small businesses, this is the owner or a senior manager.

questions.question: Which of the following data types should NEVER be put into AI tools? (Select all that apply)

answers.text: Passwords and access credentials
answers.feedback: Passwords, health information, and bank/credit card details are classified as 'Restricted' and should never be put into AI tools. Published marketing content is public, and general meeting notes without personal information are typically safe for approved business-tier tools.

answers.text: Published marketing content from your website
answers.feedback: Passwords, health information, and bank/credit card details are classified as 'Restricted' and should never be put into AI tools. Published marketing content is public, and general meeting notes without personal information are typically safe for approved business-tier tools.

answers.text: Health information about staff or customers
answers.feedback: Passwords, health information, and bank/credit card details are classified as 'Restricted' and should never be put into AI tools. Published marketing content is public, and general meeting notes without personal information are typically safe for approved business-tier tools.

answers.text: Bank account numbers and credit card details
answers.feedback: Passwords, health information, and bank/credit card details are classified as 'Restricted' and should never be put into AI tools. Published marketing content is public, and general meeting notes without personal information are typically safe for approved business-tier tools.

answers.text: General meeting notes with no personal information
answers.feedback: Passwords, health information, and bank/credit card details are classified as 'Restricted' and should never be put into AI tools. Published marketing content is public, and general meeting notes without personal information are typically safe for approved business-tier tools.

questions.question: Your team member wants to use a free AI chatbot to draft customer emails that include customer names and order details. What should you do?

answers.text: Allow it — AI chatbots are safe for all data
answers.feedback: Free-tier AI tools typically use your inputs for model training, making them inappropriate for customer data. The right approach is to use a business-tier account (which has data protection agreements) and require human review before sending customer communications.

answers.text: Ban all AI use for customer communications
answers.feedback: Free-tier AI tools typically use your inputs for model training, making them inappropriate for customer data. The right approach is to use a business-tier account (which has data protection agreements) and require human review before sending customer communications.

answers.text: Require a business-tier AI account and human review before sending
answers.feedback: Free-tier AI tools typically use your inputs for model training, making them inappropriate for customer data. The right approach is to use a business-tier account (which has data protection agreements) and require human review before sending customer communications.

answers.text: Allow it only for customers who have given written consent
answers.feedback: Free-tier AI tools typically use your inputs for model training, making them inappropriate for customer data. The right approach is to use a business-tier account (which has data protection agreements) and require human review before sending customer communications.

questions.question: What is the recommended minimum record-keeping approach for AI governance in a small business?

answers.text: Purchase dedicated governance software
answers.feedback: A well-maintained spreadsheet covering your AI register, incident log, and key decision records is perfectly adequate for most small businesses. It's simple, accessible, and meets the spirit of the MBIE guidance.

answers.text: A spreadsheet with your AI register, incident log, and decision log
answers.feedback: A well-maintained spreadsheet covering your AI register, incident log, and key decision records is perfectly adequate for most small businesses. It's simple, accessible, and meets the spirit of the MBIE guidance.

answers.text: Keep no records — NZ has no mandatory AI record-keeping laws
answers.feedback: A well-maintained spreadsheet covering your AI register, incident log, and key decision records is perfectly adequate for most small businesses. It's simple, accessible, and meets the spirit of the MBIE guidance.

answers.text: Hire an external auditor to maintain records
answers.feedback: A well-maintained spreadsheet covering your AI register, incident log, and key decision records is perfectly adequate for most small businesses. It's simple, accessible, and meets the spirit of the MBIE guidance.

questions.question: When evaluating a new AI vendor, which of the following are red flags in their terms of service? (Select all that apply)

answers.text: Your data is used to train the AI model
answers.feedback: Data being used for training, no deletion guarantees, and unilateral terms changes are all red flags. A free trial is standard practice (not a red flag), and having an SLA for uptime is a positive sign.

answers.text: The vendor offers a 14-day free trial
answers.feedback: Data being used for training, no deletion guarantees, and unilateral terms changes are all red flags. A free trial is standard practice (not a red flag), and having an SLA for uptime is a positive sign.

answers.text: No guarantee that data is deleted when you close your account
answers.feedback: Data being used for training, no deletion guarantees, and unilateral terms changes are all red flags. A free trial is standard practice (not a red flag), and having an SLA for uptime is a positive sign.

answers.text: The vendor can change terms at any time without notice
answers.feedback: Data being used for training, no deletion guarantees, and unilateral terms changes are all red flags. A free trial is standard practice (not a red flag), and having an SLA for uptime is a positive sign.

answers.text: The vendor has a Service Level Agreement for uptime
answers.feedback: Data being used for training, no deletion guarantees, and unilateral terms changes are all red flags. A free trial is standard practice (not a red flag), and having an SLA for uptime is a positive sign.

questions.question: Which AI-assisted activities require human oversight before the output reaches a customer?

answers.text: Only legal and financial outputs
answers.feedback: Human oversight should be applied to all customer-facing content, financial calculations, legal/compliance matters, and any decisions affecting people (hiring, performance). Internal brainstorming and research can have lighter oversight.

answers.text: All customer-facing content, financial calculations, legal matters, and decisions about people
answers.feedback: Human oversight should be applied to all customer-facing content, financial calculations, legal/compliance matters, and any decisions affecting people (hiring, performance). Internal brainstorming and research can have lighter oversight.

answers.text: Nothing — AI tools are accurate enough to send directly
answers.feedback: Human oversight should be applied to all customer-facing content, financial calculations, legal/compliance matters, and any decisions affecting people (hiring, performance). Internal brainstorming and research can have lighter oversight.

answers.text: Only outputs that contain personal data
answers.feedback: Human oversight should be applied to all customer-facing content, financial calculations, legal/compliance matters, and any decisions affecting people (hiring, performance). Internal brainstorming and research can have lighter oversight.

questions.question: A customer asks whether your business uses AI. What is the best approach?

answers.text: Avoid answering — there's no legal requirement to disclose AI use in NZ
answers.feedback: Transparency builds trust. While NZ doesn't have a mandatory AI disclosure law, being honest about your AI use — and explaining your safeguards like human review — positions your business as trustworthy and professional. Customers generally appreciate honesty.

answers.text: Say no to avoid losing the customer
answers.feedback: Transparency builds trust. While NZ doesn't have a mandatory AI disclosure law, being honest about your AI use — and explaining your safeguards like human review — positions your business as trustworthy and professional. Customers generally appreciate honesty.

answers.text: Be honest, explain how you use AI, and describe your human oversight process
answers.feedback: Transparency builds trust. While NZ doesn't have a mandatory AI disclosure law, being honest about your AI use — and explaining your safeguards like human review — positions your business as trustworthy and professional. Customers generally appreciate honesty.

answers.text: Refer them to your legal team
answers.feedback: Transparency builds trust. While NZ doesn't have a mandatory AI disclosure law, being honest about your AI use — and explaining your safeguards like human review — positions your business as trustworthy and professional. Customers generally appreciate honesty.
